question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user submit a token dissociate of a deleted token with a totalSupply delta that, when aggregated with the existing totalSupply via 'totalSupply + newTotalSupply', produces a value of exactly -1, which the upsert coalesce then treats as a negative value and applies the additive branch 'e_total_supply + (-1)', causing a perpetual decrement on every subsequent upsert and eventually driving the persisted totalSupply to Long.MIN_VALUE?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user cause the importer to enter an infinite retry loop by submitting a token dissociate of a deleted token that produces a totalSupply overflow in the Java layer (Long.MIN_VALUE - 1), causing setTotalSupply() to silently wrap around to Long.MAX_VALUE due to Java's unchecked long arithmetic, and then the upsert SQL to treat this as a non-negative replacement value, permanently corrupting the token's supply?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that Java long arithmetic wraps silently (no ArithmeticException for overflow unlike Math.addExact) in the expression 'totalSupply = totalSupply + newTotalSupply' to cause a wrapped-around positive totalSupply to be stored, which the upsert coalesce then treats as a non-negative replacement, permanently setting the token's supply to a large positive value that violates the maxSupply constraint and corrupts all future supply accounting?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user submit a token dissociate of a deleted token where the negative totalSupply delta is exactly Long.MIN_VALUE, causing the Java expression 'totalSupply == null ? newTotalSupply : totalSupply + newTotalSupply' to set totalSupply = Long.MIN_VALUE when totalSupply is null, and then the SQL upsert to store Long.MIN_VALUE as the supply, which when subsequently decremented by another dissociate causes a PostgreSQL bigint overflow and crashes the importer?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the mergeToken() logic where 'current.setTotalSupply(previous.getTotalSupply())' is called unconditionally at the end of the merge, overwriting any negative delta that was already accumulated in current.totalSupply via setTotalSupply(), causing the dissociate-of-deleted-token supply reduction to be silently lost and the token's supply to remain permanently inflated?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: setTotalSupply(Long)] Can an unprivileged user cause a denial of service by submitting thousands of token dissociate transactions for deleted tokens within a single record file, causing the importer to call setTotalSupply() thousands of times with negative deltas on the same in-memory Token object, accumulating a totalSupply so negative that the subsequent SQL upsert's coalesce expression produces a value that violates a database check constraint, aborting the entire batch and halting the importer?
